cards.title: 🏰 History
cards.body: Peleș Castle was built between 1873 and 1914 as a summer residence for King Carol I of Romania. Designed by architects Johannes Schultz and Karel Liman, it was one of the most modern castles in Europe at the time of its completion, featuring central heating, an elevator, and electricity from its own power plant.

cards.title: 🎨 Architecture
cards.body: A masterpiece of Neo-Renaissance architecture blended with German styles, the castle features 160 rooms, each decorated in a distinct international style — Florentine, Moorish, Turkish, French Rococo, and Louis XVI. The exterior is adorned with wood carvings, stucco, and ornate turrets overlooking the Bucegi Mountains.

cards.title: 💡 Did You Know?
cards.body: Peleș Castle was the first castle in Europe to be fully powered by electricity — long before most European cities had electric lighting. The electricity was generated by a local plant on the Peleș river. It also had one of the earliest indoor cinemas in Romania, used for royal screenings.

cards.title: 🎭 Royal Legacy
cards.body: The castle served as the summer residence for the Romanian royal family for over 70 years. It houses an impressive art collection of over 2,000 paintings, sculptures, and tapestries. After being confiscated by the communist regime in 1949, it was opened to the public as a museum and today attracts over 500,000 visitors per year.

cards.title: 📍 Visitor Info
cards.body: Peleș Castle is located in Sinaia, in the Prahova Valley, about 120 km north of Bucharest. It sits at an altitude of 800 m, surrounded by forested Carpathian hills. The Pelișor Castle, a smaller royal residence in Art Nouveau style, is located just 200 meters away and is also open to visitors.
